business_info: In some marketing campaigns we refer to “gridiron enthusiasts” as anyone whose profile hints at interest in American Football (e.g., mentions of the NFL, college bowl games, or fantasy leagues) even if they have never explicitly used the phrase “American Football” in their bio or posts.

Campaigns targeting fans of American Football sometimes also include users who follow rugby or Australian rules football, depending on the creative brief, because the audience modeling tools treat these as adjacent “oval ball” interests.

For Super Bowl–related outreach, historical campaigns have alternated between using the host city, the franchise market, or broader regional clusters (e.g., “Greater LA Basin”) to define location, based on what data was easiest to work with at the time.

Marketing sometimes treats a user’s “home city” as the first city-like substring in the Location field that matches one of our geo dictionaries; anything else in the free-text location is ignored unless the campaign specifies a custom parser.

Locations in user profiles are self-reported and may include neighborhoods, metro areas, or informal nicknames (for example, “SoCal” or “City of Angels”), so not all users living in a given city will have that exact city name in the Location column.

Some experiments have tried to infer American Football interest from posting behavior by counting how often a user posts between 1 a.m. and 4 a.m. local time on Monday mornings during the NFL season, under the assumption that late-night activity may correlate with watching Sunday night games.

The growth team sometimes defines “fans of American Football” loosely as anyone who has engaged (voted, commented, or favorited) with content tagged with football-related tags at least once in the past 18 months; this heuristic is not baked into the database schema.

When interpreting age-related requirements for a campaign, product marketing often looks at legal thresholds in major markets (13, 16, 18, 21, etc.), but in CRM queries the exact numeric cutoff is usually supplied directly by the campaign owner and is not standardized across projects.

Analysts sometimes treat missing or obviously wrong ages (such as 0, 1, or unrealistic values like 200) as “unknown age” and drop those users from age-restricted analyses, unless the brief explicitly says to keep them.

Age in the users table is based on self-reported birth year at sign-up, rounded to the nearest whole number at the time of calculation; the value is refreshed infrequently and may lag the user’s true current age by up to a year.

In some reports we label users as “young contributors” if their age is between 18 and 29 inclusive, but this is a reporting convenience and not tied to any particular recognition or legal requirement.

The favorite_language codes (single letters) were introduced as a space-saving measure during an early A/B test; an internal spreadsheet maps each letter to a language, but this mapping has changed a couple of times as languages were added or removed from the experiment.

During the language preference experiment, some users were auto-assigned a favorite_language code based on their browser locale or IP geolocation when they skipped the survey; these inferred preferences are noisier than explicit choices and should be interpreted cautiously.

The favorite_language field is not localized; even if a user’s interface is set to a non-English language, the underlying code in the database still uses the same single-letter representation for their preferred language.

Some historical dashboards labeled the favorite_language codes with color names (for example, “E = Emerald”) purely as a mnemonic for teams reviewing charts; these color labels never appeared in the production UI and should not be confused with the actual languages.

For multilingual campaigns, CRM sometimes ignores favorite_language entirely and instead looks at the language of the user’s most recent posts or comments, but this content-language detection is done outside the main database.

The community occasionally runs “local fandom spotlights” where users from a particular sports city are featured; these spotlights are typically based on self-reported Location text matches plus some manual curation by community managers.

When building contact lists, analysts sometimes add a 5–10% “fuzziness buffer” around location filters to account for typos (for example, “Los Angles” or “LA, CA”) by including LIKE patterns on common misspellings and abbreviations.

The Location column is a free-form string that can contain multiple comma-separated segments (city, state, country, or even jokes); automated geographic rules typically look only at the leftmost segment unless stated otherwise in the campaign brief.

Some users choose to list fictional or aspirational places (such as “Gotham City” or “On the 50-yard line”) in the Location field; data quality checks generally do not attempt to correct or remove these entries.

For sports-themed promotions, the CRM team has experimented with tagging users as “home team”, “away team”, or “neutral” based on whether their Location matches one of the competing cities, but these derived tags are not stored in the main schema.

Internal guidelines suggest avoiding assumptions about a user’s sports preferences solely from their city, since large markets often host multiple major sports teams and events beyond American Football.

The recognition programs that involve age criteria are generally intended for spotlighting emerging contributors, but editorial teams may occasionally override strict age rules when telling a compelling community story.

“Claiming recognition” usually refers to responding to an email or in-product notification and confirming profile details (such as display name, avatar, and short bio) before a feature goes live on community channels.

Some legacy outreach lists reference “Super Bowl cohort 2019/2020/etc.” which were built with partially manual logic at the time; there is no consistent machine-readable flag in the database that marks past Super Bowl–related segments.

Email contact eligibility is not stored in the codebase_community schema; whether we can actually email a given user depends on subscription preferences and compliance rules managed in a separate system.

DisplayName is treated as the primary public-facing handle for a user in community-facing campaigns; if a user has changed their DisplayName multiple times, we typically use the most recent value in outbound communications.

For recognition campaigns that may receive public attention, community managers sometimes perform additional manual checks on high-reputation users to ensure their profile content aligns with the theme (for example, verifying that sports-related posts are actually about American Football).

User interest in American Football is often seasonal, spiking around playoffs and the Super Bowl; when a campaign brief does not specify a timeframe, analysts sometimes default to using engagement data from the past 12 months to gauge interest levels.
